title: QUIC RFC 9001 Large Key-Update Epoch Architecture
purpose: Define the narrow runtime boundary for repeated 1-RTT key-update epoch identifiers that can advance beyond the previous 32-bit implementation ceiling while preserving the existing one-bit packet Key Phase signal.
risks: If the runtime treats the one-bit packet Key Phase signal as the epoch counter, repeated updates can stop at an artificial implementation ceiling.
If sender and recovery ledgers keep a narrower phase type than the TLS bridge, retained-old cleanup can stop matching the actual epoch.
If this slice is presented as full unbounded key-update support, it overstates the remaining RFC 9001 lifecycle proof.
design_summary: The bridge, lifecycle, sender ledger, congestion-control state, and recovery timing state keep the repeated 1-RTT key-update epoch identifier as an internal wide integer. Packet protection still emits and evaluates only the low-bit Key Phase value on the wire. Repeated local and peer installs advance the wide epoch across the old 32-bit boundary, retain the previous epoch with the same wide identifier, and keep cleanup matching the retained-old epoch in sender and recovery ledgers.
alternatives_considered: Keep the 32-bit epoch and document it as bounded support. Rejected because repeated key updates are keyed by a one-bit packet signal and the runtime should not impose a small internal epoch ceiling.

Replace the lifecycle with a general unbounded epoch ledger. Rejected because the current closure slice only needs to remove the concrete 32-bit implementation cap and preserve existing retained-old semantics.
Saturate the published epoch at 32 bits while using a hidden wide counter. Rejected because it would leave adjacent runtime code vulnerable to accidentally using the narrowed value.
edge_cases_and_constraints: The low bit of the wide epoch remains the only Key Phase value carried in packets.
Existing first-update and repeated-update cooldown gates remain unchanged.
Retained-old cleanup must use the same wide epoch identifier recorded when the old material was retained.
The slice does not add public key-update API surface, 0-RTT behavior, retry behavior, or interop claims.
data_and_state_considerations: The wide epoch flows through QuicTransportTlsBridgeState, QuicOneRttKeyUpdateLifecycle retained-old and acknowledged-current state, QuicConnectionSentPacket and retransmission records, QuicCongestionControlState sent-packet records, and QuicRecoveryTiming sent-packet records. Existing KeyPhase material derivation and packet header behavior continue to use the epoch low bit for the packet Key Phase field.
open_questions: The remaining product/runtime model for an exhaustive unbounded epoch ledger and long-lived cleanup policy is still tracked by the broader RFC 9001 key-update lifecycle gap.
